full_text:
Requirements Engineering for Quantum Software:
Challenges and Techniques
Abstract— Eliciting, specifying, and validating requirements for quantum software systems introduces
challenges that classical requirements engineering methods are ill-equipped to handle. Stakeholders often
lack the quantum computing background needed to specify correctness criteria, and formal verification of
quantum specifications is computationally intractable in the general case. We present a catalogue of
quantum-specific requirement smells and a lightweight requirements template that extends IEEE 830 with
quantum-relevant attributes: qubit budget, gate depth constraints, noise tolerance, and classical fallback
conditions. A pilot study with 12 quantum software projects validates the usefulness of the template.
1. Introduction
Requirements engineering (RE) is the first phase of software development, yet it has received little
attention in the quantum software context. We address this gap by cataloguing quantum-specific
requirement smells.
2. Requirement Smells
We identify six smells: vague qubit constraints, missing noise budget, absent classical fallback, undefined
measurement semantics, ambiguous probability thresholds, and missing hardware assumptions.
3. Template
Our IEEE 830 extension adds four quantum attributes to functional requirements.
4. Pilot Study
12 open-source quantum projects were analysed, finding an average of 3.2 smells per project.
5. Conclusion
Lightweight RE tooling can significantly improve quantum software quality.
